title: Weaning lambs at the right time
subtitle: Choosing the right moment by age and weight, managing ewe dry-off, and supporting lambs through separation.
excerpt: Weaning at the wrong time sets lambs back and stresses ewes. Both age and weight matter: a lamb that is old enough but too light will not cope as well as one that meets both thresholds. This tutorial covers how to judge readiness, how to dry off ewes without mastitis, and what to watch for in the week after separation.
sourceNotes:
AHDB Beef and Lamb, Better Returns Programme: Improving Flock Performance (2013). https://ahdb.org.uk
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Sheep (1998). https://www.gov.uk
SAC Consulting, Sheep Weaning Guidance. https://www.sac.co.uk

glossaryTerms.definition: The live weight of a lamb at the point of separation from the ewe. The minimum recommended weaning weight for lowland breeds is 14 kg live weight; hill breeds can be weaned at a lighter weight but benefit from reaching 12 kg. Lambs below these thresholds are at higher risk of set-back, worm burden, and failure to thrive after separation.

The process by which a ewe stops producing milk after weaning. Milk production should cease within two to three weeks of removing the lambs. If the udder remains hard and hot after one week, mastitis should be suspected. Feeding the ewe a reduced diet in the week before weaning helps milk production drop more gradually.

Inflammation of the udder, usually caused by bacterial infection. It presents as a hard, hot, swollen half of the udder in acute cases; chronic mastitis produces a firm lumpy udder with reduced milk output. Weaning is a risk period because milk that is not removed accumulates and provides a growth medium for bacteria. Check all ewes' udders at least twice in the week after lambs are removed.

content.text: Most commercial lowland flocks wean at 12 to 14 weeks. That age threshold is a practical starting point, not an absolute rule. The better guide is a combination of age and
. A lamb that reaches the weight threshold before 10 weeks can be weaned early if the ewe is in poor condition and struggling to keep pace with milk demand.

Weigh lambs in the week before planned weaning. Any lamb below 14 kg on a lowland system should stay with the ewe for another two weeks if she is still milking well. Lambs above threshold that are grazing actively and eating hay or creep feed will cope well with separation. Lambs that are still suckling frequently and showing little interest in grass are not ready behaviourally, even if they meet the weight threshold.
Abrupt weaning on a full diet leads to udder engorgement and
risk. In the three days before weaning, move the ewe off lush pasture onto poorer grazing or hay only. This reduces her milk output before the lambs are removed. Do not restrict water.

attrs.title: Out of sight, out of hearing
attrs.body: At separation, put ewes and lambs out of earshot if possible. Both call for 24 to 48 hours. Keeping them in adjacent fields prolongs the distress period significantly. The safest method for ewes is to move the lambs away rather than removing the ewes, so the ewes stay on familiar ground.

content.text: Check each ewe's udder for signs of

every day for the first week. A normal drying udder is slightly full on day one and softer by day three. An udder that is hot, hard, or painful after three days warrants a vet call. Early mastitis treated promptly usually resolves; ignored mastitis in one half of the udder can lead to the permanent loss of that side.

The first week after weaning is the highest-risk period for worm burden in lambs. Their immune response to gut worms is still maturing, and the stress of weaning depresses it further. Carry out a faecal egg count five to seven days after weaning and treat if the result is above threshold. Move lambs to a clean pasture at weaning if possible; a field that has not carried sheep for at least three months carries a lower worm burden than the ewes' field.
Check all lambs are eating and drinking on day one after separation.
Look for scouring, which can develop rapidly in stressed lambs on lush summer pasture.
Weigh lambs at two weeks post-weaning. Any lamb that has lost weight needs a worm count and a condition assessment.
